coredata.dc:title: Gully-like CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composite with broad spectral response and its enhanced photocatalytic performance

coredata.dc:description: The morphology and structure of photocatalytic materials often have a great influence on their photocatalytic performance. The photocatalytic degradation and hydrogen production capacity can be enhanced by optimizing the morphology and properties of the catalysts. In this paper, on the basis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with up-conversion photoluminescence (UCPL) properties and polystyrene colloid microspheres (PS) as templates, a kind of gully-like CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composites with wide spectral response was prepared by vacuum impregnation and microwave-assisted hydrothermal method. The results show that CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composites with PS colloid microsphere as templates have a gully structure, which provides more active sites for photocatalytic reaction. The heterostructure formed among components optimizes the charge transfer path, significantly improving the separation and transfer efficiency of photogenerated electrons and holes. In the multi-mode photocatalytic experiments, CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composites show excellent photocatalytic activity for methyl orange, of which photocatalytic activity is much higher than that of pure TiO2, displaying a certain degree of degradation for the different kinds of dyes. In addition, the hydrogen evolution capacity of CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composites is 1149.3 μmol/g for 8 h, which is 7 times of that of monomer TiO2. The results of the capture experiments show that the possible photocatalytic mechanism of CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composites is more likely to be the result of a synergy between the traditional band theory and the "Z-scheme".

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The morphology and structure of photocatalytic materials often have a great influence on their photocatalytic performance. The photocatalytic degradation and hydrogen production capacity can be enhanced by optimizing the morphology and properties of the catalysts. In this paper, on the basis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with up-conversion photoluminescence (UCPL) properties and polystyrene colloid microspheres (PS) as templates, a kind of gully-like CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composites with wide spectral response was prepared by vacuum impregnation and microwave-assisted hydrothermal method. The results show that CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composites with PS colloid microsphere as templates have a gully structure, which provides more active sites for photocatalytic reaction. The heterostructure formed among components optimizes the charge transfer path, significantly improving the separation and transfer efficiency of photogenerated electrons and holes. In the multi-mode photocatalytic experiments, CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composites show excellent photocatalytic activity for methyl orange, of which photocatalytic activity is much higher than that of pure TiO2, displaying a certain degree of degradation for the different kinds of dyes. In addition, the hydrogen evolution capacity of CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composites is 1149.3 μmol/g for 8 h, which is 7 times of that of monomer TiO2. The results of the capture experiments show that the possible photocatalytic mechanism of CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composites is more likely to be the result of a synergy between the traditional band theory and the "Z-scheme".

The unique up-conversion characteristic of CQDs broadened the spectrum absorption range of gully-like CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composites, and the heterostructure between In2O3 and TiO2 accelerated the electron transfer, which jointly promoted the degradation of organic pollutants and the efficiency of photo-splitting water for hydrogen production.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Traditional fossil fuels have not been able to meet the requirements of maintaining sustainable social development and improving human living standards. Therefore, it is necessary to develop renewable and green energy sources to replace fossil fuels [1,2]. Semiconductor photocatalysis, as an emerging remediation technology for purifying water and air, has received widespread attention. TiO2 is widely used in the field of photocatalysis due to its advantages of non-toxicity, good chemical stability and high photo-corrosion resistivity. However, TiO2 has a wide band gap (3.0–3.2eV) and can only be excited under ultraviolet light with a wavelength lower than 387 nm, which greatly limits the practical application of TiO2 in many fields [3]. Therefore, a large number of modification strategies, such as precious metal deposition, metal or nonmetal doping, controlled morphology, and coupling with narrow band gap semiconductors or carbon materials with π-conjugated structures, are aimed at overcoming the above shortcomings and thereby improving TiO2 photocatalytic performance [4–9].

Among them, combining with another narrow-band gap semiconductor to form heterojunction is an effective way to simultaneously enhance the light capture capability and promote TiO2 photoinduced carrier separation [10–14]. In2O3, an indirect semiconductor with a band gap of 2.56 eV, is generally regarded as a highly efficient sensitizer that can extend the absorption spectrum of transition metal oxide photocatalysts from ultraviolet light to the visible region [15,16]. In addition, due to the matching of strip arrangement between TiO2 and In2O3, a type-II heterojunction photocatalytic system can be formed between them, which can significantly improve the separation and transfer of photogenerated carriers [17,18]. So far, various types of TiO2/In2O3 hybrid photocatalysts have been constructed for photocatalytic organic matter decomposition and water splitting [18–20]. Siriluck et al. [21] studied the photocatalytic degradation of Congo red by changing the molar ratio of In2O3 to TiO2 and roasting temperature of the mesoporous assembled In2O3–TiO2 composite oxide catalyst. The results showed that when the molar ratio of In2O3–TiO2 was 0.05:0.95, of which degradation activity was significantly higher than that of TiO2 powder. Yang et al. [22] synthesized In2O3–TiO2 hybrid nanoribbons for photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue under visible light. The results show that the enhancement of their photocatalytic activity can be attributed to the obtained heterostructures.

In recent years, CQDs has been widely used to prepare a series of broad-spectrum responsive photocatalytic composites due to its unique up-conversion characteristics. Research results show that the composite material loading with CQDs can improve the utilization rate of sunlight to a certain extent [23]. Pan et al. [24] prepared CQDs/TiO2 nanotube composites for the degradation of rhodamine B (RhB) under visible light irradiation, the results showed that the degradation effect of CQDs/TiO2 on RhB was much higher than that of pure TiO2. Qiu et al. [25] modified CQDs on BiOCl0.5Br0.5, which greatly improved the separation of photogenerated carriers and enhanced the photocatalytic activity. Liu et al. [26] modified CQDs on porous carbonitriding titanium dioxide nanosheets (CQD-pCN/TNs). Under visible light irradiation for 120 min, the degradation rate of tetracycline by CQD-pCN/TNs was 86.2 %. The enhancement of photocatalytic activity of the composite was due to the expansion of absorption wavelength range caused by CQDs modification and the inhibition of electron–hole recombination by heterojunction structure in the composite. Obviously, efficient photocatalytic materials with broad spectrum response can be obtained by applying CQDs with up-conversion characteristics [27].

In addition, a large number of studies have shown that the morphology and structure of photocatalytic materials also have a great influence on their photocatalytic activity. Compared with nanoparticles, one-dimensional (nanowires, nanorods), two-dimensional (nanosheets) and three-dimensional hierarchical structures have the advantages of rapid transfer of photogenerated carriers and higher specific surface area of photocatalytic materials [28].

In this paper, we successfully synthesized CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composites with broad spectral response using PS microspheres as the template by colloidal template and microwave-assisted hydrothermal methods. The prepared CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composites combine the excellent properties of CQDs, In2O3 and TiO2, in which CQDs as electron collectors prolong the carrier lifetime due to the unique up-conversion characteristics, and the heterojunction formed between In2O3 and TiO2 increases the electron transfer and thus improves the photocatalytic performance. The gully morphology of the composites increases the active sites of the reaction, and thereby further enhancing the photocatalytic performance of the photocatalyst.
Monodisperse PS microspheres were prepared by emulsifier-free emulsion polymerization [28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs with up-conversion characteristics
ce:section.ce:para: 2 g glucose and trace amount of NaOH were added into 30 mL of deionized water, stirred until all the particles were dissolved, and continued to stir for 30 min until the solution was colorless and transparent. Microwave reaction was maintained at 150 °C for 30 min. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained brown solution was dialyzed for 24 h. The dialysate was dried at 50 °C for 12 h, ethanol solution was added, and the brown flocculent was ultra-sounded until it appeared. The product was centrifuged and washed with ethanol for three times, and then dried at 50 °C for 12 h to obtain CQDs powder.

The triblock copolymer (EO20PO70O20 (P123)) was dissolved in isopropyl alcohol, then appropriate amount of Titanium isopropoxide (TTIP) was added to the solution, and deionized water was added to form a gel. The resulting gel was then dried in a vacuum drying oven. Finally, the completely dried solid mixture was calcined in Muffle furnace to obtain the required pure TiO2.

After centrifugal processing of PS colloidal spheres solution, it was added to an aqueous solution of TTIP and indium nitrate (In(NO3)3) at room temperature with continuous stirring and the addition of an appropriate amount of deionized water until the appearance of a gel-like substance. The obtained gel was dried in a vacuum drying oven at 50 °C for 11 h. Finally, the obtained solid was calcined at 500 °C for 8 h to obtain the required In2O3/TiO2 composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composites of different proportions
ce:section.ce:para: 0.2 g of In2O3/TiO2 composites were put into 30 mL of deionized water and stirred in an ultrasonic bath for 15 min until the solution appears as a suspension. Afterwards, 0.001, 0.002, 0.004 g of CQDs powders were weighed and added into the above mixture separately, followed by sonicating for 30 min. Each suspension was then heated to 120 °C in a polytetrafluoroethylene reactor by a microwave reaction and maintained for 30 min. The obtained solids were washed three times with water and ethanol respectively, and dried at 50 °C for 12 h to obtain the final sample.
For other relevant experimental information, please refer to the supplementary materials and Figs. S1 and S2.

In order to determine the crystalline structure of the synthesized CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composites, X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was carried out on CQDs, In2O3/TiO2 and CQDs/In2O3/TiO2. As shown in Fig. 1 , after calcination at 500 °C for 8 h, diffraction peaks of In2O3/TiO2 composite materials are mainly located at 25.33°, 37.80°, 38.05°, 48.16° and 53.85°, which is consistent with the (101), (004), (112), (200) and (105) crystal faces of anatase phase TiO2 (JCPDS 21–1272), respectively [29,30]. In addition, In2O3/TiO2 composite has a weak diffraction peak at 30.58°, which is consistent with the (222) crystal face of In2O3(JSPDS 06–0416) [31]. Compared with TiO2, the diffraction peak of In2O3 is relatively weak and not very obvious, it is the reason that the amount of In2O3 in the composites is relatively small and the degree of dispersion is high. According to the below HR-TEM result, there is still evidence that In2O3 exists in the composites. By comparing CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composites with In2O3/TiO2 composites, it is clear that the characteristic peaks do not change significantly after loading with CQDs. This is because, on the one hand, CQDs are composed of discrete carbon nanoparticles with small size, on the other hand, the low content of CQDs, accounting for 1 % of the mass of CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composites, is not easy to be detected by XRD analysis [32,33].

In order to study the optical absorption properties of the prepared photocatalytic material CQDs/In2O3/TiO2, UV–vis diffuse reflection (UV–vis/DRS) absorption spectra of different samples were analyzed [34]. Fig. 2 (a) is the light absorption spectra of different materials and Fig. 2(b) shows the different energy plot curves. As can be seen from Fig. 2(a), TiO2 has strong absorption in the ultraviolet region and non-obvious absorption in the visible region. Although the effect of In2O3 on TiO2 is not particularly large when In2O3 and TiO2 combine, In2O3/TiO2 still has a certain degree of redshift compared with TiO2, and its absorption in the visible light region is also enhanced to a certain extent. In addition, the redshift of CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 is further obvious, and with the increase of CQDs loading, the absorption in the visible region gradually increases, which can be attributed to the up-conversion effect of CQDs and its unique performance of effectively absorbing photogenerated electrons [35].

Based on the optical absorption results of the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectrum of the products, the bandgap of the monomer was calculated according to Kubelka–Munk formula:
(1)
αhv=A(hv-Eg)
n/2 [36]
where α represents the absorption coefficient; n stands for light frequency; A is the proportionality constant. By plotting a graph between (αhν)
n and hν based on the spectral responses, shown in Fig. 2(b). Thus, based on Fig. 2(b), the Eg values of TiO2 and In2O3 are evaluated to be about 3.20 eV and 2.80 eV, respectively.

In order to determine the surface morphology and element distribution information of the composite, SEM and EDS analyses were performed, and the results are shown in Fig. 3 . It can be seen from Fig. 3(a) and Fig. S3 that 1 % CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composite material presents a semi-cylindrical gully-like morphology and the dimensions of each gully are basically the same, which can be attributed to the directional accumulation of PS microspheres. The particles deposited on the gully surface are mainly CQDs and a small amount of In2O3, and the main part of the gully structure is TiO2. Moreover, nanoparticles are coated on the gully surface of the composite, which makes the surface rough, and thus enlarge the specific surface area of the composite to a certain extent and increase the binding sites between the composites and the reaction (model) molecules.

The successful preparation of CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composite is further proved by analyzing the existence of each element by EDS. EDS mapping of 1 % CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composite given in Fig. 3 (b) shows there are four elements with the mass ratios of 2.48 %, 1.45 %, 49.12 % and 46.95 % for C, In, Ti and O, respectively. Through the observation of element mapping images in Fig. 3(c, d), C and In elements show uniformly dispersed in the composite material, which proves the existence of CQDs and In2O3 in the composite material. Since the proportion of In2O3 and CQDs In the CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composite is very small, the signal of C and In elements is not obvious, but the presence of C and In elements in the CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composite can also be confirmed by follow-up XPS analysis results. In addition, it can be seen from Fig. 3(e and f) that a large number of Ti elements and O elements are distributed in composite materials, which indicates that the main body of the composite is still TiO2.

To further investigate the detailed microstructure information of CQDS/In2O3/TiO2 composite, TEM and HR-TEM analysis were performed and the results are shown in Fig. 4 . As shown in Fig. 4(a), the composite has a gully structure, which is consistent with the results of SEM. At the same time, it can be seen from Fig. 4(b) that CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composites have lattice stripes of CQDs, TiO2 and In2O3, indicating that the gully CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composites have been successfully synthesized. Among them, the lattice fringe spacing of about 0.32 nm in Fig. 4(c) is attributed to the (002) crystal face of CQDs, which further proves the existence of CQDs in the composite material [37]. The lattice fringe spacing of about 0.29 nm in Fig. 4(d) is attributed to the (222) crystal face of In2O3 [38]. The lattice fringe spacing of about 0.35 nm in Fig. 4(e) is well matched with the (101) crystal surface of TiO2 [39]. HR-TEM analysis shows that the crystal faces of each component in the composite are consistent with those of XRD. In addition, the overlap of lattice fringe surfaces of CQDs, In2O3, and TiO2 indicates a strong bonding effect among three components.

The HR-TEM analysis shows that the strong effect among CQDs, In2O3 and TiO2 optimizes the separation and transfer path of photogenerated electrons and holes, which is expected to improve the photocatalytic properties of the composite.

In order to study the chemical valence states of the elements on the surface of the composite material, XPS tests were performed on 1 % CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composites, and the results are shown in Fig. 5 .

As can be seen from Fig. 5(a), there are mainly four elements C, In, Ti and O on the surface of the composite, which is consistent with the element mapping spectra of the composite material. Fig. 5(b) shows the XPS spectrum of C 1s in 1 % CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composites, in which 284.8 eV peaks are attributed to foreign carbon sources, and peaks at 286.3 eV and 288.5 eV correspond to C–O–C and CC bonds, respectively, proving the existence of CQDs [40]. Fig. 5(c) shows the XPS spectrum of In 3d in 1 % CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composites. The electron binding energy of In corresponds to 444.8 eV and 452.3 eV, respectively, which proves that the element exists in the form of an In3+ ion [41]. The XPS spectrum of Ti 2p in 1 % CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composites given in Fig. 5(d) shows electron binding energies correspond to 458.6 eV and 464.3 eV, indicating the presence of Ti2+ ions on the surface of the composites [42].

In addition, Fig. 5(e) shows the XPS spectrum of O 1s in 1 % CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composites, whose electron binding energies are 529.9 eV and 531.1 eV, corresponding to the characteristic peaks of lattice oxygen and hydroxyl oxygen, respectively [43].

In order to study the surface physicochemical properties of composite materials, N2 adsorption–desorption was performed on monomer TiO2, In2O3/TiO2, 0.5 % CQDs/In2O3/TiO2, 1 % CQDs/In2O3/TiO2, and 2 % CQDs/In2O3/TiO2. The results are shown in Fig. 6 and Table 1 . As shown in Fig. 6, the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of the synthesized series of CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composites belong to the type IV adsorption curve type. According to the definition of IUPAC [44], they all belong to typical mesoporous structures. Monomer TiO2 has H1 type lag ring, while all composite materials display H3 type lag ring.

According to the data in Table 1, the specific surface area of In2O3/TiO2 composite is significantly larger than that of monomer TiO2, which is due to the fact that the two composites form a gully structure, and the gully structure can increase the specific surface area. With the loading of CQDs, the specific surface area of the composite firstly increases and then decreases. Since the size of CQDs is too small, CQDs effectively modify the surface of the composites and thus increase the specific surface area. However, additional load may block the pore channel of some parts of the composite materials, resulting in a decreasing specific surface area.

In order to detect the up-conversion characteristics of the prepared CQDs, PL tests were carried out on the CQDs solution after dialysis. As shown in Fig. 7 (a), CQDs is excited with light of 650–900 nm, and CQDs converts it into visible light of 380–600 nm. Moreover, the main peak wavelength and peak intensity of the emission spectrum vary with the wavelength of the excitation light source, that is, the up-conversion emission spectrum is obviously dependent on the wavelength of the excitation light source. As the wavelength of the excitation source increases from 650 nm to 900 nm, the intensity of the emission spectrum changes from weak to strong and then to weak. In this paper, UV–vis/DRS results show that the light absorption band edge of In2O3/TiO2 is at 410 nm, which indicates that the light energy emitted by CQDs up-conversion is well utilized by In2O3/TiO2. Therefore, visible and near-infrared light in the solar spectrum can be effectively utilized with the up-conversion effect of CQDs.

To study the photogenic carrier separation and transfer efficiency of CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composites, photoluminescence (PL) spectra, transient photocurrent and Nyquist (EIS) spectra were analyzed. The photoluminescence spectra at an excitation wavelength of 300 nm are shown in Fig. 7(b). In the figure, the luminous intensity of monomer TiO2, In2O3/TiO2 and 1 % CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 decreases successively, indicating that with the composite of In2O3 and the modification of CQDs, the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes in the composite is significantly inhibited. The separation and transfer efficiency of photogenerated electron–hole pairs are gradually enhanced [45].

In order to further study the e−–h+ pair separation ability of the sample, we conducted a photocurrent transient response test on the sample, and the results are shown in Fig. 7(c). Obviously, compared with pure TiO2 and In2O3/TiO2, the photocurrent density of 1 % CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 is significantly enhanced, which means that photogenerated charge carriers have a higher concentration, proving that the formation of heterojunction structure in the composite material. In addition, the separation and recombination of photogenerated electrons and hole pairs in the composite were further studied by Nyquist Atlas, and the results are shown in Fig. 7(d). In general, the smaller the diameter of the Nyquist graph, the lower the charge transfer resistance [46]. It can be seen from Fig. 7(d) that 1 % CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 has the shortest radius, indicating that it has the lowest charge transfer resistance. Above results have been further confirmed in the following photocatalytic activity experiments.

In order to study the photocatalytic performance of CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 series materials, a series of multi-mode photocatalytic experiments were carried out under ultraviolet light, visible light and simulated sunlight with methyl orange as the model molecule. Meanwhile, the direct photolysis in this paper refers to the degradation effect of light on methyl orange without adding any photocatalysts, which is used as a blank sample for reference in photocatalytic experiments, and the results are shown in Fig. 8 and Fig. S4. It can be seen that, 1 % CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composite has the highest degradation efficiency for methyl orange compared with other materials, which can be attributed to the strong light absorption capacity of pure TiO2 in the ultraviolet region. In addition, after the composite of In2O3, the synergistic effect between TiO2 and In2O3 further improves the degradation efficiency of the catalyst. After continuing to load CQDs, the recombination rate of photogenerated electrons and holes in the system decreases, which is also the reason for the obvious improvement of the degradation efficiency of the photocatalyst. The catalytic activity is obviously enhanced, which is basically consistent with the previous results of UV–vis/DRS absorption spectroscopy. In order to understand the influence and action of different catalysts on the degradation of methyl orange, the reaction kinetics is carried out, and the calculation formula is:
(2)

It is obvious from Fig. 8(b) and Fig. S4(b) that 1/C

0 has a linear relationship with the reaction time t, indicating that the degradation of dye (methyl orange) by different catalysts follows a quasi-second-order reaction kinetics. In order to measure the suitability of the composite material for degradation of organic pollutants, different dyes were degraded under ultraviolet light irradiation. It can be seen from Fig. 8(c) that 1 % CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composites have the best degradation rate for methyl orange and certain degree of degradation effect on other dyes and salicylic acid, indicating that 1 % CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composites have relatively broad applications.

In addition, under the irradiation of visible light and simulated sunlight, 1 % CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composites show the best photocatalytic activity, as shown in Fig. 9 . Under visible light irradiation, the degradation rate of methyl orange is 4 times that of monomer TiO2. Under the irradiation of simulated sunlight, the degradation rate of methyl orange is more than 3.7 times that of monomer TiO2. This is the result of the interaction between In2O3/TiO2 heterojunction and CQD, which enhances the visible light absorption and the separation efficiency of photogenerated electrons and holes. In addition, the morphology increases the active site on the catalyst surface, thus improving the photocatalytic activity. The above results show that 1 % CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composite material prepared in this study can significantly improve the utilization rate of sunlight.

In order to determine the optimal hydrogen evolution performance of the photocatalytic material with the addition of sacrificial agents, methanol-aqueous solution (volume ratio = 1:10), triethanolamine (TEOA)-aqueous solution (volume ratio = 1:10) and Na2S aqueous solution (0.1 M) were selected and compared with no sacrificial agent. Fig. 10 (a) shows the hydrogen evolution of 1 % CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 under different sacrificial agent systems. After irradiation by xenon lamp for 8 h, the minimum hydrogen evolution of the composite in aqueous solution is 538.2 μmol/g without adding sacrificial agent, while TEOA-aqueous solution exhibits the highest hydrogen evolution capacity of 1149.3 μmol/g, indicating that the hydrogen production capacity of this photocatalytic material is related to the selection of sacrificial agents. Fig. 10(b) shows the hydrogen evolution results of different catalysts in TEOA-aqueous solution system. It can be seen from Fig. 10(b) that the hydrogen evolution volume of TiO2 is 162.6 μmol/g. After combining with In2O3, the hydrogen production performance of In2O3/TiO2 composite is obviously improved, and the hydrogen evolution capacity reaches 590.6 μmol/g, which is over 3 times that of monomer TiO2. Obviously, the rational use of band gap engineering can significantly improve the hydrogen evolution activity of the material. Moreover, in order to further enhance the hydrogen evolution capacity, different amounts of CQDs are added. With the increase of CQDs, the hydrogen evolution capacity of the photocatalyst gradually increases. When the adding amount of CQDs is 1 %, the hydrogen evolution capacity reaches 1149.3 μmol/g, which is 7 times that of monomer TiO2. However, further increase of CQDs reduces the hydrogen evolution volume, which may be because the excessive amount of CQDs covers on the surface of the catalyst, and the coated catalyst could not be photoexcited, resulting in the decline of photocatalytic hydrogen evolution capacity.

The above results further show that the modification of CQDs with up-conversion characteristics not only significantly improves the utilization rate of sunlight, but also greatly increases the transfer efficiency of photogenerated electrons through reasonable design of band gap engineering and the ability of CQDs to absorb electrons, and therefore, the hydrogen evolution activity is effectively enhanced.

In addition, in order to investigate the stability of the composite, a cyclic experiment was carried out. As shown in Fig. 10(c), the hydrogen evolution amount of 1 % CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composites still reaches 1039.4 μmol/g after four cycles, indicating that it has good stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible photocatalytic reaction mechanism of CQDs/In2O3/TiO2
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the photocatalytic reaction mechanism of gully-like 1 % CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composite, the active substances in the photocatalytic reaction were captured and determined by adding different trapping agents. As shown in Fig. S5, the degradation rate of methyl orange decreases significantly after different trapping agents are added under the same intensity of ultraviolet light. Among them, the addition of superoxide radical trapping agent p-benzoquinone (BQ) exhibits the largest reduction, indicating that the main active factor in the photocatalysis of 1 % CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 is superoxide free radical (·O2 −). After methanol or triethanolamine are added, the degradation rate of methyl orange comes down by half, proving that superoxide radical plays the main role and hydroxyl radical (·OH) and hole (h+) play the auxiliary roles in the photodegradation process.

According to the above experimental results, and the following formula:
(3)
E
CB = χ -E
e -0.5E
g
(4)
E
VB = E
CB + E
g

The conduction band edge potential (ECB) and valence band edge potential (EVB) of In2O3 and TiO2 are calculated by formulas (3) and (4). Where ECB is the band gap edge of CB, χ is the geometric mean of the electronegativity of the atoms that make up the semiconductor system, and Ee is about 4.5 eV. The calculated conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) positions of the semiconductor are shown in Table 2 , as well as the possible photocatalytic reaction mechanism of CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 (Fig. 11).
Based on the above results and relevant experimental data, we speculated the possible photocatalytic reaction mechanism of 1 % CQDs/In2O3/TiO2, as shown in Fig. 11 .

Under simulated sunlight irradiation, CQDs act as spectral converters to improve the utilization of solar energy. The up-conversion characteristics of CQDs can convert long-wavelength light (λ1) to short-wavelength light (λ2), broaden the optical response range of the composite, and promote In2O3 and TiO2 to reach the excited state more easily. According to the traditional band structure theory, since the conduction band (CB) of In2O3 (CB = −0.62 eV) is more negative than that of TiO2 (CB = −0.25 eV), photogenerated electrons transfer to the conduction band of TiO2, and the electrons on the conduction band of TiO2 react with dissolved oxygen in water to form ·O2−, and thus degrade dye molecules. In the process of photocatalytic hydrogen production, the electrons on the conduction band of TiO2 reduce the water to H2. The valence band (VB) of TiO2 (VB = 2.95 eV) is more positive than the valence band of In2O3 (CB = 2.18 eV), so the holes transfer to the valence band of In2O3. Since the valence band of In2O3 at 2.18 eV is more negative than the redox potential of ·OH formation (2.4 eV vs NHE), the valence band of In2O3 may not generate ·OH, which is inconsistent with the captured active species. Therefore, a possible photocatalytic mechanism in this study is more inclined to the synergistic effect of traditional band theory and "Z-scheme" [47].

First, under simulated sunlight, the gully structure can increase light absorption to improve the utilization of solar energy. Then, due to the up-conversion characteristics of CQDs, long-wavelength light (λ1) is converted to short-wavelength light (λ2), and thus the optical response range of the composite is broadened, making In2O3 and TiO2 more easily to reach the excited state. In addition, CQDs, as an electron transfer mediator and acceptor, have the properties of accelerating electron transfer rate and absorbing electrons, which increase the electron conduction path in photocatalysis, extend the lifetime of photogenerated electrons, and reduce the recombination probability of photogenerated electron–hole. 1 % CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composite material is excited by the converted light, and the light excitation of In2O3 and TiO2 makes the electron transition from the valence band to the conduction band. Some photogenerated electrons are transferred according to the traditional electron conduction path: electrons are transferred from CB of In2O3 to CB of TiO2, and the converged electrons can not only react with oxygen to form ·O2 −, which can degrade methyl orange into CO2 and H2O, but also reduce water to H2. The holes on the VB of TiO2 are transferred to the VB of In2O3, and the holes on the VB of In2O3 can directly oxidize methyl orange, resulting in its final mineralization to H2O and CO2. In the process of hydrogen evolution, TEOA reacts with holes to form TEOAOX. Another part of photogenerated carriers in the recombination process work as electron acceptors through CQDs, so that the electrons on CB of TiO2 and the holes on VB of In2O3 recombine quickly. At the same time, CQDs on CB of In2O3, as electron acceptors, can accumulate a large number of electrons and react with oxygen to generate ·O2 −, which degrade methyl orange to CO2 and H2O and also reduce water to H2. The holes on the VB of TiO2 also react with water to generate ·OH, and finally mineralize the dye molecules into H2O, CO2 and inorganic ions. Through the synergistic effect of traditional energy band theory and "Z-scheme", the possible photocatalytic mechanism of the composite material is photodegradation and photolysis water to produce hydrogen by three different active species, which corresponds to the results of the capture experiments above.

In summary, 1 % CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composites with gully-like structure can not only significantly improve the solar energy utilization rate, but also increase the electron transfer and inhibit photogenerated electron–hole recombination under the synergistic effect of traditional energy band theory and "Z-scheme".

In this paper, based on PS colloidal microspheres as templates, the gully-like CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composites with wide spectral response were successfully prepared by sol–gel and microwave-assisted hydrothermal methods. The unique gully structure enlarges the specific surface area of the composite and increases the active site of the reaction. CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composites use the up-conversion characteristics of CQDs to convert long-wavelength light into short-wavelength light, thereby improving the utilization rate of the composite in the visible light region. In addition, CQDs, as electron acceptor and medium, can improve the total quantum efficiency of photocatalysis system. Due to the synergistic effect among CQDs, In2O3 and TiO2, the photocatalytic properties of CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 composites are improved by effectively preventing the recombination of photogenerated electron–hole pairs. Among them, 1 % CQDs/In2O3/TiO2 shows the best photodegradation and hydrogen production activity. Therefore, this paper provides a simple method for enhancing the solar energy utilization rate and promoting the photocatalytic performance of wide-band gap semiconductors.
